title: Your First curl
subtitle: Send a real HTTP request from the terminal

sections.content:
`curl` is your walkie-talkie to the internet.

You speak (send a request), the server answers (sends a response). No browser, no app, no abstraction — just you and the raw conversation.

Every developer uses `curl` to test APIs, debug requests, and check if a server is alive. It's one of the most useful tools you'll ever learn.
keyPoints: `curl <url>` sends a GET request and prints the response body
`curl -i <url>` includes response headers above the body
`curl -s <url>` is silent mode — no progress bar
The response body is usually JSON for API endpoints

sections.instruction: Let's make your first HTTP request. You're about to talk directly to a server — no browser required.
sections.hint: Type the command exactly as shown, then press Enter

onSuccess.message: You got a response: `{"message": "Hello from the internet!"}` — that's JSON. The server received your GET request, processed it, and sent back data. This is how every API interaction works.

sections.instruction: Now let's see the **full** response — including the headers. Add the `-i` flag (i = include headers).
sections.hint: Add -i before the URL: curl -i https://...

onSuccess.message:
Now you see the full picture:

`HTTP/2 200` — the status line (version + code)
`content-type: application/json` — what format the body is in
`date:` — when the server responded

Then a blank line, then the body.

This is the raw conversation between your terminal and the server.

completionMessage: You just made your first HTTP request from the terminal. No browser. No app. Just curl — your direct line to any server on the internet.
